publications.title: Structural and kinetic basis for heightened immunogenicity of T cell vaccines.
publications.authors: Chen JL, Stewart-Jones G, Bossi G, Lissin NM, Wooldridge L, Choi EM, Held G, Dunbar PR, Esnouf RM, Sami M, Boulter JM, Rizkallah P, Renner C, Sewell A, van der Merwe PA, Jakobsen BK, Griffiths G, Jones EY, Cerundolo V.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined how peptide modifications enhance T cell vaccine immunogenicity using the HLA-A2-restricted NY-ESO-1(157-165) tumor epitope and its cognate TCR. A crystal structure of the wild-type peptide–TCR–HLA-A2 complex revealed that TCR recognition is centered on two sequential peptide residues (methionine and tryptophan), and a single C9V anchor substitution that increases MHC binding affinity also subtly repositions the peptide backbone to enhance TCR interactions. The modified analogue peptide shows improved soluble TCR binding and elicits faster lytic-granule polarization, reduced CD8 dependence, and broader cross-reactive CTL responses than the native peptide. These findings demonstrate that rational incorporation of structural data into peptide optimization can generate superagonist variants with enhanced immunogenicity for cancer immunotherapy, with implications for T cell vaccine design.

publications.title: Directed evolution of human T cell receptor CDR2 residues by phage display dramatically enhances affinity for cognate peptide-MHC without increasing apparent cross-reactivity.
publications.journal: Protein science : a publication of the Protein Society
publications.authors: Dunn SM, Rizkallah PJ, Baston E, Mahon T, Cameron B, Moysey R, Gao F, Sami M, Boulter J, Li Y, Jakobsen BK.
publications.executive_summary: This study engineered the CDR2 regions of a human T cell receptor through phage display to dramatically enhance binding affinity for a cognate peptide-MHC complex while maintaining specificity—a counterintuitive result, since CDR2 loops typically contact only the MHC surface rather than the peptide itself. The engineered low-nanomolar-affinity TCRs retained high specificity for their target pMHC, with structural analysis revealing that improved shape complementarity at the CDR2-MHC contact interface drove the affinity gain. This finding challenges the expectation that increasing TCR affinity would necessarily increase off-target cross-reactivity, suggesting instead that careful mutagenesis of germline-encoded CDR2 loops can improve binding without sacrificing recognition selectivity. The work has direct implications for TCR engineering approaches in therapeutic and diagnostic applications, indicating that the MHC-contact surface—not just peptide-contact regions—offers a tractable avenue for generating high-affinity, specific TCRs suitable for clinical use.

publications.title: Crystal structures of high affinity human T-cell receptors bound to peptide major histocompatibility complex reveal native diagonal binding geometry.
publications.authors: Sami M, Rizkallah PJ, Dunn S, Molloy P, Moysey R, Vuidepot A, Baston E, Todorov P, Li Y, Gao F, Boulter JM, Jakobsen BK.
publications.executive_summary: The study presents crystal structures of three in vitro-engineered, high-affinity TCRs recognizing the tumor-associated peptide NY-ESO(157-165) (SLLMWITQC) presented on MHC-I, alongside comparison with the parental wild-type TCR. Despite achieving affinities substantially higher than natural TCRs (which typically range 1–100 μM), the engineered TCRs maintain the same diagonal binding geometry and overall docking mode as the wild-type, with affinity gains arising from CDR2 and CDR3 mutations that work synergistically to increase interface area, improve shape complementarity (particularly for the central methionine and tryptophan peptide residues), and add hydrogen-bonding interactions. The structures reveal an induced-fit mechanism in which CDR3 loop restructuring enables better peptide recognition and binding. These findings are relevant to TCR engineering for cancer immunotherapy, demonstrating that affinity enhancement for tumor neoantigens can be achieved within the constraints of the conserved TCR-MHC binding geometry, informing rational design strategies for therapeutic TCR variants.

publications.title: High-throughput peptide-MHC complex generation and kinetic screenings of TCRs with peptide-receptive HLA-A*02:01 molecules.
publications.authors: Moritz A, Anjanappa R, Wagner C, Bunk S, Hofmann M, Pszolla G, Saikia A, Garcia-Alai M, Meijers R, Rammensee HG, Springer S, Maurer D.
publications.executive_summary: This study developed a disulfide-stabilized HLA-A*02:01 molecule that remains stable without peptide ligand, enabling efficient one-step loading with peptides of choice to generate pMHC complexes for structural and functional analysis. The researchers characterized wild-type and affinity-matured TCR binding to these engineered pMHCs using crystal structure determination and kinetic measurements, demonstrating that the engineered HLA variant behaves similarly to wild-type with respect to TCR recognition. They established a high-throughput platform to measure binding kinetics of bispecific TCRs (bsTCRs) against diverse pMHC libraries and showed that bsTCR affinity for pMHCs correlates with in vitro function, while generating a binding motif to predict off-target interactions in the human proteome. This platform is particularly relevant for cancer immunotherapy applications targeting tumor-associated neoantigens, as it enables rapid safety and efficacy screening of engineered TCR therapeutics against both intended and unintended pMHC targets.
